summary: Graham argues that startups should seek technical advantage by choosing the most powerful tools available, and that in Viaweb this meant using Lisp to build faster and better software than competitors. He contends that language choice matters because more powerful languages enable kinds of solutions that are impractical elsewhere, and that competitors’ habits often blind them to such advantages.
key_points: Startups cannot succeed by doing what the average startup does; they need an odd, leverage-heavy advantage.
Web-based software frees teams to choose any language, making language choice a strategic weapon.
Lisp’s power (especially macros) enabled Viaweb’s rapid development and unique features.
The “Blub paradox” explains why programmers underestimate more powerful languages they don’t know.
Keeping technical advantages opaque can preserve a competitive edge.
money_quotes: If you do everything the way the average startup does it, you should expect average performance.
In business, there is nothing more valuable than a technical advantage your competitors don't understand.
Programming languages are not just technology, but what programmers think in.
By induction, the only programmers in a position to see all the differences in power between the various languages are those who understand the most powerful one.
In business, as in war, surprise is worth as much as force.
key_insight: Choosing a more powerful language can give a startup a decisive, compounding advantage because it accelerates development and enables capabilities competitors cannot easily match.
